skill.name: github-issues
skill.description: Manage GitHub issues for the Logseq Template Graph project with structured triage, validation, labeling, response drafting, implementation planning, and PR coordination.
skill.summary: Structured GitHub issue triage and lifecycle guidance for Logseq Template Graph maintainers.
skill.author: C0ntr0lledCha0s

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many high-risk pattern matches, but targeted review found Markdown examples and maintainer workflow guidance rather than hidden executable code or malicious intent. The skill is publishable with a medium warning because it guides state-changing gh and git commands and includes limited external documentation lookup.

medium_findings.title: State-Changing GitHub and Git Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill provides legitimate maintainer workflows, but it repeatedly instructs use of gh and git commands that can label, comment on, close issues, create branches, push changes, and open pull requests. This is not malicious, but users should review commands before execution because they can modify repository and GitHub state.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections directly show gh issue, gh pr, git checkout, git push, and related commands. The intent is repository maintenance rather than malware, so the risk is operational and permission-dependent.

low_findings.title: Static External-Command Matches Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The static analyzer labeled many Markdown backticks as Ruby or shell execution. Review found fenced examples and inline command references, not executable Ruby code or bundled scripts. The commands are still operational guidance, so the real concern is user-approved command execution rather than hidden code execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected locations are Markdown command examples for grep, git, and gh. No executable source file or Ruby backtick evaluation is present in the skill files.

low_findings.title: Weak-Crypto and Reconnaissance Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography hits correspond to ordinary words such as issue workflow text, labels, or placeholder commit hashes, not MD5, SHA1, or cryptographic implementation. The reconnaissance hits are project-maintenance searches like grep, ls, git log, and gh issue list used to validate reports.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review found placeholders and maintainer validation commands, not cryptographic code or host/network scanning. Legitimate repository inspection is plausible and consistent with the skill purpose.

low_findings.title: Limited External Documentation Lookup
low_findings.description: The only network indicator reviewed is a Schema.org reference used to validate a property definition. This is expected for issue triage, but users should avoid sending private issue contents to external sites unless needed.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited references point to documentation research, not exfiltration. Risk remains because external lookup may expose context if copied into requests.

dangerous_patterns.title: State-Changing GitHub and Git Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill provides legitimate maintainer workflows, but it repeatedly instructs use of gh and git commands that can label, comment on, close issues, create branches, push changes, and open pull requests. This is not malicious, but users should review commands before execution because they can modify repository and GitHub state.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections directly show gh issue, gh pr, git checkout, git push, and related commands. The intent is repository maintenance rather than malware, so the risk is operational and permission-dependent.

content.user_title: Manage GitHub Issues with Structured Triage
content.value_statement: Maintainers often lose time validating reports, finding duplicates, and writing consistent updates. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a repeatable workflow for triage, labels, responses, and PR coordination.
actual_capabilities: Guides issue validation by checking code, history, duplicates, and related reports.
Suggests labels for issue type, priority, status, and project scope.
Drafts maintainer responses for bugs, feature requests, questions, and duplicates.
Creates implementation plans with affected files, dependencies, and next steps.
Coordinates GitHub issue actions such as comments, closures, labels, and linked pull requests.
Provides reusable examples for common Logseq Template Graph issue workflows.
limitations: Focused on the Logseq Template Graph project and its labels, modules, and issue style.
Requires GitHub CLI access for commands that read or modify GitHub issues.
Does not verify command safety automatically before external state changes.
Schema.org research and project validation may require current repository and network access.

use_cases.title: Triage incoming bug reports
use_cases.description: Validate reproduction details, check whether the bug is already fixed, and draft a clear maintainer response.

use_cases.title: Plan feature implementation
use_cases.description: Assess a feature request, identify affected template files, map dependencies, and prepare a staged implementation plan.

use_cases.title: Clean up duplicate issues
use_cases.description: Search existing issues, compare reports, add duplicate labels, and prepare closure comments with links to canonical issues.

prompt_templates.title: Triage a new issue
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner workflow for a single issue
prompt_templates.prompt: Review issue #[number]. Validate whether it is a bug, feature request, question, or duplicate. Suggest labels and draft a response.

prompt_templates.title: Find duplicates and related work
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze issue #[number] against open and closed issues. Identify duplicates, related issues, blockers, and recommended relationship comments.

prompt_templates.title: Create an implementation plan
prompt_templates.prompt: For issue #[number], validate the request, identify affected modules, list implementation tasks, estimate effort, and draft a progress comment.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate issue to pull request
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare the issue lifecycle plan for #[number], including branch name, PR title, closing keywords, labels, release note text, and final issue comment.

output_examples.input: Issue says the Recipe class is missing.
output_examples.output: The response explains that Recipe already exists, cites the implementation location and release, labels the issue as duplicate, and closes with update instructions.

output_examples.input: Issue requests a new MedicalCondition class.
output_examples.output: The output identifies the missing medical module dependency, proposes a phased plan, adds blocked status, and links related foundation work.

output_examples.input: Issue asks how to use Person for contact management.
output_examples.output: The answer points to existing documentation, gives a concise usage summary, applies a question label, and closes after answering.

best_practices: Validate every report against code, history, and existing issues before responding.
Review all gh and git commands before running actions that change repository state.
Include evidence, relationships, labels, and next steps in each maintainer response.
anti_patterns: Closing issues without explaining the validation result or linking supporting evidence.
Running generated commands against a repository before reviewing their target issue numbers.
Assuming a reporter is correct without checking code, documentation, or duplicate issues.

faq.question: Does this skill execute GitHub commands by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides command guidance and response drafts. The user or host tool controls command execution.

faq.question: Which GitHub tool does it expect?
faq.answer: It expects the GitHub CLI, usually the gh command, to read and update issues or pull requests.

faq.question: Can it work outside Logseq Template Graph?
faq.answer: Some triage ideas transfer, but labels, file paths, modules, and examples are tailored to Logseq Template Graph.

faq.question: Can it create pull requests?
faq.answer: It can guide branch, commit, push, and pull request steps when implementation is complete and authorized.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but it includes commands that can change GitHub issues, branches, and pull requests.

faq.question: What should users review before using it?
faq.answer: Review issue numbers, repository names, labels, comments, branch names, and any network lookup before running commands.
